metadata.title: HTE: The Average Effect Hides the Subgroup Story
metadata.topic: CAUSAL MACHINE LEARNING — HETEROGENEOUS TREATMENT EFFECTS

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The scatter where biomarker score drove CATE — top quartile 0.23 versus bottom 0.06 around an average of 0.12 — is exactly the subgroup story I keep missing. And the permutation test at p=0.002 is what makes it defensible. I want to run that gate myself. Show me: (1) fitting a T-learner and splitting CATEs into biomarker quartiles; (2) ranking every feature by its interaction strength with treatment to find the true moderator; (3) running a placebo-shuffle permutation test and reporting how often the quartile gap survives.

props.command: The scatter where biomarker score drove CATE — top quartile 0.23 versus bottom 0.06 around an average of 0.12 — is exactly the subgroup story I keep missing. And the permutation test at p=0.002 is what makes it defensible. I want to run that gate myself. Show me: (1) fitting a T-learner and splitting CATEs into biomarker quartiles; (2) ranking every feature by its interaction strength with treatment to find the true moderator; (3) running a placebo-shuffle permutation test and reporting how often the quartile gap survives.
props.topic: CAUSAL MACHINE LEARNING — HETEROGENEOUS TREATMENT EFFECTS
props.segment: HTE: The Average Effect Hides the

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Average treatment effect = 2 percentage points. But for one biomarker segment it is 15. For another it is negative. The average is hiding the story.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'HTE — the average is a lie'; subgroup heterogeneity hiding under ATE

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Fit a T-learner on synthetic RCT data, compute individual CATEs, split into quartiles by biomarker, and identify which feature best separates high-benefit from low-benefit patients.

props.command: claude "Write a Python HTE analysis: synthetic RCT n=2000, features: age (30-80), biomarker (0-1), comorbidity (binary). True CATE = 0.2*biomarker - 0.003*age + 0.1. Treatment T~Bernoulli(0.5). Fit T-learner (GradientBoosting). Compute individual CATEs. Split top/bottom quartile by predicted CATE. Print mean CATE, mean Y|T=1, mean Y|T=0 for each quartile."

beats.narration_text: Read it. The HTE analysis bridges population-level evidence to individual-level decisions. The causal forest estimates heterogeneity; the subgroup analysis makes it actionable.

props.code:
# hte_analysis.py — Claude Code output

# True CATE varies by biomarker
# CATE(x) = 0.2 * biomarker - 0.003 * age + 0.1

# T-learner: two separate GBM models
m1 = GBM().fit(X[T==1], Y[T==1])
m0 = GBM().fit(X[T==0], Y[T==0])
cate_pred = m1.predict(X) - m0.predict(X)

# Quartile split by predicted CATE
q_labels = pd.qcut(cate_pred, q=4, labels=['Q1','Q2','Q3','Q4'])
# Top quartile: biomarker dominant predictor of benefit

beats.narration_text: The scatter plot reveals the story: biomarker score drives CATE. Top quartile mean CATE is 0.23 — nearly four times the bottom quartile mean of 0.06. The average effect of 0.12 hides both.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: scatter plot biomarker vs predicted CATE colored by benefit quartile

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to add a CATE forest permutation test — does the heterogeneity survive a placebo shuffle? If the top-bottom quartile gap vanishes under permuted treatment, the HTE is real.

props.command:
claude "update hte_analysis.py:
  -> add permutation test: shuffle treatment label 200 times
  -> recompute top-bottom CATE quartile gap each time
  -> report p-value: fraction of permutations exceeding observed gap"

beats.narration_text: Permutation p-value of 0.002 — the top-bottom quartile gap occurs in fewer than 0.2 percent of placebo shuffles. The heterogeneity is not noise. The biomarker is a genuine treatment effect modifier.
beats.visual_intent: slate: permutation test histogram; observed gap vs null distribution

beats.narration_text: HTE analysis is three steps: estimate individual CATEs, sort by predicted CATE, validate with permutation or calibration. The ATE is just the mean of the distribution. The distribution is the story.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: three-step HTE workflow; ATE vs CATE distribution

beats.narration_text: Your move: take your last RCT and compute individual CATEs. Find the feature with the highest interaction with treatment. That is your subgroup moderator. The next reel covers policy learning — how to turn those CATEs into optimal treatment rules.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: run HTE, find moderator, proceed to policy learning reel

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. HTE analysis is three steps: estimate individual CATEs, sort by predicted CATE, validate with permutation or calibration. The ATE is just the mean of the distribution. The distribution is the story. Your move: take your last RCT and compute individual CATEs. Find the feature with the highest interaction with treatment. That is your subgroup moderator. The next reel covers policy learning — how to turn those CATEs into optimal treatment rules.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [HTE: The Average Effect Hides the Subgroup Story] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
